target.name: Copenhagen Atomics
target.founder: Thomas Jam Pedersen, Peter Szabo, Thomas Steenberg, Aslak Stubsgaard

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $25M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Nuclear Energy

private.name: Fuse Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Nuclear Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M+

data.narrative:
Copenhagen Atomics is a Series A nuclear energy company that has raised $25M+ to date. The nuclear energy sector encompasses 3 public peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private companies including Fuse Energy, Oklo, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power, though no sufficiently comparable narrow peer cohort exists at identical stage and valuation tier.

Given the lack of three or more direct comparables in the same stage and tier, the Peer-Median approach was not applicable. Instead, a Stage-Progression method was employed, using the $25M total raised as a proxy for post-money valuation and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of progression from Series A to the subsequent round. This yields an implied valuation of $50M.

The triangulation supports a median valuation of $50M, with a range of $42M (low) to $57M (high). The analysis rests primarily on a single method, which represents a material limitation; the primary uncertainty concerns whether the 2.0x multiplier accurately reflects investor step-ups in the nuclear energy sector specifically, versus broader venture patterns. Additional uncertainty stems from the nascent and heterogeneous nature of the private nuclear sector, making historical progression data less reliable than in mature industries. A narrower range would require either disclosure of comparable recent Series A/B rounds in nuclear or explicit market signaling from current or prospective investors.
